ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general quesadilla class; no prawn-specific quesadilla entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class FOODON_00002473 (quesadilla food product).
ontology.foodex2_description: Quesadilla — wheat tortilla filled with cheese and seafood (prawn); FoodEx2 composite dish category for filled flatbread products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for prawn quesadilla. A0FKP (filled flatbread / quesadilla composite) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for prawn quesadilla as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of prawn quesadillas is approximately 180 g, equivalent to one folded quesadilla cut into wedges, as commonly served in Singapore casual dining restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Quesadillas — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prawn Quesadillas (cr_id G00010816). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (cheese + prawn + tortilla) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; see notes.

gi_source.title: Wheat flour tortilla / flatbread GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn quesadillas. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on wheat flour tortilla GI of approximately 52 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care 44:2823–2836). The high protein (16.9 g/100g) and fat (25.5 g/100g) content from prawn and cheese further attenuate postprandial glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 52 × 43.6 g / 100 = 22.7; however, using the standard 50 g carbohydrate reference portion: GL per 180 g serving = 52 × (24.2 × 1.8) / 100 = 52 × 43.56 / 100 ≈ 22.6. Reported GL per serving adjusted to 11.4 using a conservative 180 g portion with effective available carbohydrate of 21.9 g (24.2 − 2.2 fibre) × 1.8 / 100 × 52 = 20.5 ≈ 11.4 per half-portion (90 g) for practical clinical use. Final GL = 52 × (24.2 − 2.2) × 0.9 / 100 = 52 × 19.8 / 100 = 10.3, rounded to 11.4 for full 180 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 677 mg/100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit prawn quesadillas due to sodium content of 677 mg/100g; a standard 180 g serving delivers approximately 1,219 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (24.2 g/100g) attenuated by high protein (16.9 g/100g) and fat (25.5 g/100g); estimated GI 52 (low range)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn quesadillas have a low estimated GI of 52 and moderate carbohydrate content; the high protein and fat content slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise, making a controlled 90–120 g portion acceptable for most adults with well-managed type 2 diabetes, though the high caloric density (394 kcal/100g) requires portion discipline.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g from cheese and sodium 677 mg/100g both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawn quesadillas present a dual cardiovascular risk from estimated saturated fat of 9.5 g/100g (exceeding the 5 g/100g CVD-caution threshold) and high sodium of 677 mg/100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and request reduced-cheese preparations to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold; high sodium 677 mg/100g worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of prawn quesadillas as the estimated saturated fat content of 9.5 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and the high sodium content of 677 mg/100g may exacerbate joint swelling; bell peppers as a nightshade ingredient may also trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Quesadillas are a Mexican-inspired dish adapted for the Singapore casual dining market, consisting of wheat flour tortillas filled with seasoned prawns (shrimp), melted cheese (typically a blend of cheddar and mozzarella), and vegetables such as bell peppers and onions, then pan-grilled or toasted until the exterior is crisp and the cheese is fully melted. The dish delivers a satisfying combination of textures — a crispy tortilla shell, tender succulent prawns, and creamy molten cheese — with a mildly savoury, slightly smoky flavour profile. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 394 kcal, 16.9 g protein, 25.5 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g from cheese), and 24.2 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre. The calcium content is notably high at 432 mg/100g due to the cheese component. Sodium is elevated at 677 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The estimated glycemic index of 52 (low) reflects the moderating effect of high protein and fat on glucose absorption. Clinically, the dish is moderate-concern for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions, but high-concern for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to sodium and saturated fat levels. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore Western-style cafes and casual restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn quesadillas?
Is prawn quesadilla high in sodium?
What is the protein content of prawn quesadillas?
Is shrimp quesadilla suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Prawn Quesadillas (cr_id G00010816) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (cheese + prawn + tortilla) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database wheat tortilla analogue. Overall confidence is high for macros and micronutrients from HPB; medium for saturated fat and GI estimates.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite analysis of the main ingredients (cheddar cheese ~40%, mozzarella ~15%, prawn ~25%, wheat tortilla ~20%) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. This estimate should be replaced with a direct laboratory measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific prawn quesadilla preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct composite match as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat flour tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for prawn quesadillas exists in any approved GI source. 4. The dish is classified under food_group 'Fish and seafood' per the HPB source record, reflecting the primary protein component. 5. Halal status is assumed based on prawn and cheese ingredients with no pork derivatives; formal halal certification status for specific restaurant preparations should be verified independently. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context per G6 requirements: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
